ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chapati entry but does not specifically represent chapati served with fish curry as a composite dish; 'closest_parent' match used for the chapati component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers chapati as a flatbread; the composite dish with fish curry is not separately coded. 'closest_parent' match applied to the dominant grain component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chapati with fish curry as a composite dish.

serving.description: 2 chapatis with a serving of fish curry
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of 2 chapatis accompanied by a portion of fish curry or fried fish, totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati with Fish — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database lists 'Chapati W Fish' (G00010342) as a canonical entry with per-100g nutrient values. Core macros, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced from HPB at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are supplemented from a secondary source at confidence 0.70; these are retained as supporting values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chapati / Whole Wheat Flatbread — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Am J Clin Nutr
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'chapati with fish curry' as a composite dish. GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati (GI ~52, low category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Fish curry contributes negligible additional carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 43.1 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 101.0 g GL for the 450 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1935 mg sodium, representing 84% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one chapati and request reduced-salt fish curry preparation; the potassium content (250 mg/100 g) provides partial offset but does not neutralise the sodium burden at typical serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is 43.1 g/100 g; however, the whole wheat fibre (6.0 g/100 g) and fish protein (12.3 g/100 g) moderate the glycemic response, and the GI of the chapati component is approximately 52 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to one chapati per meal, pair with a generous fish curry portion to leverage protein-mediated glucose attenuation, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the high glycemic load at full serving size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 430 mg/100 g is elevated; saturated fat at 2.22 g/100 g is moderate; however, fish omega-3 fatty acids and whole wheat fibre provide cardioprotective benefits.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose oily fish (mackerel, sardine) in the curry to maximise omega-3 intake, use light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat, and reduce added salt to bring sodium below 300 mg/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3-rich fish and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) are beneficial; sodium at 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis benefit from the omega-3 and polyphenol content of this dish but should reduce salt in preparation and monitor symptoms if nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) are included in the curry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Fish Curry is a popular Singaporean Indian dish consisting of whole wheat unleavened flatbread (chapati) served alongside a spiced fish curry or fried fish. The chapati is made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a small amount of oil or ghee, rolled thin and cooked on a dry tawa (griddle) until lightly charred. The fish curry is typically prepared with mackerel, snapper, or other local fish simmered in a sauce of onion, tomato, coconut milk, tamarind, turmeric, ginger, garlic, and chilli. The dish has a soft, slightly chewy flatbread texture contrasted with a rich, tangy, mildly spiced curry. Nutritionally, it provides 254 kcal/100 g with 12.3 g protein, 43.1 g carbohydrate, 6.0 g dietary fibre, and 3.55 g fat. The high fibre content from whole wheat moderates glycemic response (GI ~52). Sodium is elevated at 430 mg/100 g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The fish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, supporting cardiovascular and joint health. This dish is halal, gluten-containing, and commonly consumed at lunch and dinner in Singapore's Indian community and hawker centres.

confidence_reason: Core macros, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, and iron are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical entry G00010342 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are supplemented from a secondary source at 0.70 confidence and are clearly documented. The HPB entry is an exact match for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry G00010342 or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent chapati-with-fish-curry composite as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no equivalent composite entry was found in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010342). trans_fat_g is 0.04 g/100 g per secondary source. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as (52 × 43.1 × 4.5) / 100 = 100.9, rounded to 101.0.
